title: Recursive Midpoint Diffusion
subtitle: Brownian Lightning for Text Generation — endpoints first, details last

children.children: Lightning bolts are made by setting two endpoints and recursively displacing midpoints with noise. What if text generation worked the same way? This tutorial explores a radical architecture that generates text from the outside in — global structure first, local details last — with embarrassing parallelism at every level.

Computer graphics has a beautiful algorithm for generating lightning bolts and fractal terrain: midpoint displacement. You start with two endpoints, find the midpoint, displace it randomly, then recurse on each half. After a few levels, you get a jagged, natural-looking bolt.

props.analogy: Imagine planning a road trip. First you pick start (New York) and end (San Francisco). Then you pick a midpoint city (Denver). Then midpoints of each leg (Pittsburgh, Salt Lake City). Then smaller stops between those. You plan the BIG structure first, then fill in details. You never start driving blind from New York hoping to end up in San Francisco.

children.children: Autoregressive text generation (GPT-style) is like starting to drive from New York with no map — each token depends only on previous tokens, with no guarantee of arriving at a coherent destination. Flat diffusion is like teleporting random molecules and hoping they assemble into a road. Recursive midpoint generation gives you the map FIRST.

props.title: ⚡ Lightning Bolt Generator
2. The Algorithm: Text as a Binary Tree

children.children: Here's the core idea applied to text generation:
Goal: Generate a story from 'John is healthy' to 'John is in the hospital'.
Level 0 (Endpoints): [John is healthy] ←———→ [John is in the hospital]
Level 1 (Midpoint): [John is healthy] → [John gets hit by a car] → [John is in the hospital]
Level 2 (Fill halves): [healthy] → [crosses street carelessly] → [hit by car] → [ambulance arrives] → [hospital]
Level 3+: Each gap gets filled with increasingly specific detail until we reach actual sentences and words.
Notice: the midpoint between 'healthy' and 'hospital' MUST involve some harm event. The causal structure emerges automatically from the endpoints!

Autoregressive generation (GPT) takes O(n) steps — one per token, sequentially. A 10,000-token essay needs 10,000 sequential steps. Recursive midpoint generation takes O(log n) levels of recursion. After just 14 levels, you've planned 16,384 positions. At each level, ALL midpoint computations are independent — pure parallelism.
This is the same speedup that makes binary search faster than linear search, or merge sort faster than bubble sort. It's one of the most powerful ideas in computer science applied to text generation.

props.title: 🌳 Build a Narrative Tree

children.children: 🔀 Embarrassingly Parallel

At each recursion level, every midpoint computation is independent. Level 2 has 2 midpoints to compute — they can run on 2 different GPUs simultaneously. Level 10 has 1,024 midpoints — send them to 1,024 workers. This is a MapReduce problem: map = generate midpoint from two anchors, reduce = concatenate in order.

This would be the first text generation architecture that is truly distributable across machines. Autoregressive models are inherently sequential. Flat diffusion needs global attention at each step. Recursive midpoint generation has a clean binary tree dependency graph.
🎯 Natural Global Coherence

Global structure is locked in at Level 0–1 (endpoints and major turning points). Local details at Level 5+ can never violate the global plan. This solves the main weakness of flat diffusion models, which can lose global coherence during the denoising process because everything is refined simultaneously.
🔬 SAE Feature Hierarchy = Recursion Schedule

Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) features naturally organize into abstraction levels — coarse features (topic, genre) and fine features (word choice, syntax). The recursion levels map perfectly: Level 0 activates coarse SAE features, deeper levels activate increasingly fine-grained features. The feature abstraction ladder IS the recursion schedule.

Schankian operators define state transitions: entity_before → event → entity_after. At the coarsest recursion level, the 'endpoints' are entity states and the 'midpoint' is the event that transforms one into the other. 'John is healthy' → HARM event → 'John is injured'. The recursive refinement mirrors how narratives work: abstract summary first, then increasingly specific detail. This IS operator decomposition.

props.title: 📊 Generation Paradigm Comparison

children.children: Three major extensions were discovered: the algorithm is substrate-agnostic (works without SAEs or diffusion), separates planning from writing (like human authors), and produces an inspectable/editable generation tree.
🔧 Substrate-Agnostic: Build It Today

The recursion works with ANY anchor representation — SAE features (research version), summary phrases (practical version), plain embeddings, or even single words. A prototype can be built TODAY with vanilla LLM calls: (1) generate midpoint summary from two endpoint summaries, (2) estimate token budget per interval, (3) fill tokens given left context + right-side target.
Prompt 1 (Plan): 'Given these two story beats: [A] and [B], what is the most likely event that connects them? Reply with one sentence.'
Prompt 2 (Budget): 'This story segment covers [event]. Estimate how many words it needs: 50/100/200/500.'
Prompt 3 (Write): 'Continue this story. Previous text: [left context]. This section should end with [right anchor]. Write ~[budget] words.'
That's it — recursion + 3 prompts = hierarchical text generation. No custom models, no diffusion, no SAEs needed.
✍️ Parallel Planning, Sequential Execution

Pure parallel infilling produces incoherent seams between chunks. The solution: parallelize Phase 1 (plan the tree — cheap, just summaries) and serialize Phase 2 (write prose left-to-right, each chunk sees full left context + right anchor). This separates PLANNING from WRITING — exactly how human writers work. Outline first (hierarchical, can jump around), prose second (sequential, flowing).

props.analogy: Think of a film production. Phase 1 is the writer's room: they plan the story arc, major beats, scene outlines — all in parallel, jumping around, reorganizing. Phase 2 is the shoot: scenes are filmed in a logical order so actors have continuity. The planning is hierarchical and parallel; the execution is sequential and grounded.

children.children: 🌳 The Generation Tree as Collaboration Interface

Unlike autoregressive generation (opaque token stream) or flat diffusion (noise → text), recursive midpoint generation 'shows its work' at every level. Each level is human-readable: Level 0 = premise/conclusion, Level 1 = major turning points, Level 2 = paragraph topics. A human can inspect, edit, or override any anchor at any level, and only the subtree below regenerates.

This is not just interpretability — it's a human-AI co-authoring interface. The human engages at whatever abstraction level they want: architect the whole story at Level 1, micromanage a tricky transition at Level 5, leave the rest to the machine. No existing writing tool works this way.
The full research version has four components:

props.title: 🏗️ Architecture Components

children.children: Lightning bolt generation works because electricity DOES flow between endpoints — the midpoint displacement is physically motivated. Similarly, text flows from premise to conclusion — the semantic interpolation is linguistically motivated. The bounded Brownian walk prevents 'drift' — early choices at coarse levels constrain fine-level choices, unlike flat diffusion where early denoising errors cascade into incoherence.

SSD-LM (Semi-autoregressive Simplex-based Diffusion) generates blocks then fills gaps — similar spirit but not recursive. dLLMs show that non-autoregressive models exploit asymmetry for planning — recursive midpoint is an alternative planning solution. Steerling-8B (Guide Labs, Feb 2026) demonstrates interpretable concept routing in diffusion LMs — those routed concepts could serve as the anchor features at each recursion level. Hierarchical diffusion exists for images (cascaded models like Imagen) but this recursive midpoint structure hasn't been applied to text.

Three potential papers: (1) 'Recursive Midpoint Planning for Long-Form Generation' — LLM-only, practical, buildable today. (2) 'Recursive Midpoint Diffusion with SAE Feature Hierarchies' — the full research version. (3) 'The Generation Tree: Human-AI Collaborative Writing' — the HCI/tool paper.

props.question: Why does recursive midpoint generation have better global coherence than flat diffusion?
options: Global structure is set first at Level 0-1 and constrains all later levels
It generates more tokens per step
It uses a larger context window
props.explanation: The key insight is hierarchical constraint: endpoints and major turning points are locked in first, and all finer-grained details must be consistent with them. Flat diffusion refines everything simultaneously, so there's no guarantee that local changes preserve global structure.

props.question: What is the generation depth (number of sequential steps) for a 10,000-token document using recursive midpoint generation?
props.explanation: log₂(10,000) ≈ 13.3, so about 14 levels of recursion. At each level, all midpoints are computed in parallel. This is dramatically fewer sequential steps than autoregressive (10,000) or flat diffusion (~1,000).

props.question: What makes this architecture 'substrate-agnostic'?
options: It only works with SAE features
It requires a specific diffusion model
The recursive midpoint structure works with any anchor representation — SAE features, summaries, embeddings, or plain text
props.explanation: The insight is the recursive planning structure itself, not the specific representation. You can prototype it today with plain LLM calls generating summary-level midpoints, or build the full version with SAE features and diffusion. The algorithm is the same either way.
